[[en:Mesuriyama Kofun]] {{wikidata link|Q11343632}} [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:History of Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Sakurai, Nara]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Mesuriyama Kofun]][[en:Mesuriyama Kofun]][[ja:メスリ山古墳]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Mesuriyama Kofun]][[en:Mesuriyama Kofun]][[ja:メスリ山古墳]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Mesuriyama Kofun]][[en:Mesuriyama Kofun]][[ja:メスリ山古墳]] {{Short description|Kofun period keyhole-shaped Kofun in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] | name =Mesuriyama Kofun | native_name =メスリ山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Mesuriyama Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Mesuriyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Nara Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Mesuriyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34|29|56|N|135|50|52|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = {{ill|Sakurai, Nara|en|Sakurai, Nara|WD=Q127026|lt=Sakurai, Nara}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} | region = {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region}} | type = Kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.4th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|{{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}}}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|29|56|N|135|50|52|E}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Mesuriyama Kofun'''|メスリ山古墳|}} is an early [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofun]], located in the Takada neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Sakurai, Nara|en|Sakurai, Nara|WD=Q127026|lt=Sakurai, Nara}} in the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1980.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2036|title=メスリ山古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref>
==Overview== The Mesuriyama Kofun is located on the left bank of the Hatsuse River and belongs to the Torimiyama Kofun Cluster along with the [[Sakurai Chausuyama Kofun]], which has a separate National Historic Site designation. The Mesuriyama Kofun was excavated from 1959 to 1962. The tumulus is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It is orientated to the west, and has a total length of 224 meters. The posterior circular portion has a diameter of 128 meters and height of 19 meters. The anterior rectangular portion has a width of 80 meters at the front and a height of 8 meters. The slopes of the three-tiered mound is covered with head-sized ''[[fukiishi]]'' roofing stones, and rows of ''{{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa}}'' in between each tier. In the center of the circular mound is a double square section, with huge ''haniwa'' clay figures in the shape of high stands and vessel platforms arranged perpendicular to the main axis of the mound, and a staggered arrangement of ''haniwa'' clay figures between the two rows, creating a distinctive layout. These pedestal-type ''haniwa'' are 2.4 meters high and 1.3 meters in diameter, and are the largest in Japan. Inside this row of ''haniwa'' clay figures is a square platform, beneath which is the main [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]], a pit-style stone chamber.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The burial chamber is approximately 8.06 meters long, 1.18 meters wide, and 1.76 meters high, and is covered by eight ceiling stones. A wooden coffin was placed in the burial chamber, but due to grave robbery, almost no remains remain. {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods}} unearthed in the main chamber included fragments of a {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|WD=Q1041984|lt=Bronze mirror}} (a triangular-rimmed divine-animal mirror), jadeite ''[[magatama]]'', jasper tubular beads, stone bracelets imitating shell rings, miniature stone chairs, combs, and bowls. The secondary burial chamber next to the main chamber is 6 meters long, 70 centimeters wide, and 60-70 centimeters high. There were no remains inside, only grave goods, and it is thought to have been a storage facility or a repository for the personal belongings of the deceased. It escaped grave robbery. many artifacts were found to be in perfect preservation.<ref name="Shiseki"/> These grave goods included 212 tipped iron spears, about half of which were shaped so that their tips faced each other at either end of the stone chamber. All of them are thought to have had long handles. They were weapons used in group battles. Iron spearheads with iron sword-shaped tips have also been excavated in the southern part of the {{ill|Korea|en|Korea|WD=Q18097|lt=Korea}} and in northern [[Kyushu]], and were the main weapon of the time. This weapon was very popular in the [[Japanese archipelago]], and there is no doubt that forging techniques were also used in Japan. The hoard also included 236 bronze arrowheads, 50 stone arrowheads, one iron bow (182 cm long, with an iron string), five iron arrows (80 cm long), and a lacquered shield. The iron bow and arrows were intended for intimidation purposes, not for practical use. Two iron swords, 14 axes, a 19 iron sickles, a chisel, and agricultural tools such as an awl, a knife, and a saw were also among the grave goods. These artifacts have been collectively designated as a {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}}.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/202763|title=奈良県メスリ山古墳出土品|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref>
<gallery> Mesuriyama kohun aerial.jpg|Aerial view File:Mesuriyama Kofun, funcho.jpg|The top of the circular mound File:Mesuriyama Kofun, kouenbu.jpg|View of the circular mound from the front File:Mesuriyama Kofun, zenpoubu.jpg|View of the front part from the circular mound メスリ山古墳出土 大型円筒埴輪-2.JPG|Large cylindrical ''haniwa'' at the Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute </gallery>
Judging from the size of the mound, the size of the ''haniwa'', and the richness of the grave goods, it is believed to be the tomb of a chieftain with great power, but there us no mention of this ''kofun'' as being a royal tomb in historical documentation such as the ''[[Kojiki]]'' or ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tumulus is about 1.7 kilometers due south from {{ill|Sakurai Station (Nara)|en|Sakurai Station (Nara)|WD=Q5361714|lt=Sakurai Station}} on the {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|en|Kintetsu Railway|WD=Q1531085|lt=Kintetsu Railway}} {{ill|Osaka Line|en|Osaka Line|WD=Q1052545|lt=Osaka Line}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)|WD=Q17050860|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)}}

In the center of the circular mound is a double square section, with huge ''haniwa'' clay figures in the shape of high stands and vessel platforms arranged perpendicular to the main axis of the mound, and a staggered arrangement of ''haniwa'' clay figures between the two rows, creating a distinctive layout. These pedestal-type ''haniwa'' are 2.4 meters high and 1.3 meters in diameter, and are the largest in Japan. Inside this row of ''haniwa'' clay figures is a square platform, beneath which is the main [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]], a pit-style stone chamber.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The burial chamber is approximately 8.06 meters long, 1.18 meters wide, and 1.76 meters high, and is covered by eight ceiling stones. A wooden coffin was placed in the burial chamber, but due to grave robbery, almost no remains remain. {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods}} unearthed in the main chamber included fragments of a {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|WD=Q1041984|lt=Bronze mirror}} (a triangular-rimmed divine-animal mirror), jadeite ''[[magatama]]'', jasper tubular beads, stone bracelets imitating shell rings, miniature stone chairs, combs, and bowls. The secondary burial chamber next to the main chamber is 6 meters long, 70 centimeters wide, and 60-70 centimeters high. There were no remains inside, only grave goods, and it is thought to have been a storage facility or a repository for the personal belongings of the deceased. It escaped grave robbery. many artifacts were found to be in perfect preservation.<ref name="Shiseki"/> These grave goods included 212 tipped iron spears, about half of which were shaped so that their tips faced each other at either end of the stone chamber. All of them are thought to have had long handles. They were weapons used in group battles. Iron spearheads with iron sword-shaped tips have also been excavated in the southern part of the {{ill|Korea|en|Korea|WD=Q18097|lt=Korea}} and in northern [[Kyushu]], and were the main weapon of the time. This weapon was very popular in the [[Japanese archipelago]], and there is no doubt that forging techniques were also used in Japan. The hoard also included 236 bronze arrowheads, 50 stone arrowheads, one iron bow (182 cm long, with an iron string), five iron arrows (80 cm long), and a lacquered shield. The iron bow and arrows were intended for intimidation purposes, not for practical use. Two iron swords, 14 axes, a 19 iron sickles, a chisel, and agricultural tools such as an awl, a knife, and a saw were also among the grave goods. These artifacts have been collectively designated as a {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}}.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/202763|title=奈良県メスリ山古墳出土品|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref>
<gallery> Mesuriyama kohun aerial.jpg|Aerial view File:Mesuriyama Kofun, funcho.jpg|The top of the circular mound File:Mesuriyama Kofun, kouenbu.jpg|View of the circular mound from the front File:Mesuriyama Kofun, zenpoubu.jpg|View of the front part from the circular mound メスリ山古墳出土 大型円筒埴輪-2.JPG|Large cylindrical ''haniwa'' at the Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute </gallery>
Judging from the size of the mound, the size of the ''haniwa'', and the richness of the grave goods, it is believed to be the tomb of a chieftain with great power, but there us no mention of this ''kofun'' as being a royal tomb in historical documentation such as the ''[[Kojiki]]'' or ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tumulus is about 1.7 kilometers due south from {{ill|Sakurai Station (Nara)|en|Sakurai Station (Nara)|WD=Q5361714|lt=Sakurai Station}} on the {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|en|Kintetsu Railway|WD=Q1531085|lt=Kintetsu Railway}} {{ill|Osaka Line|en|Osaka Line|WD=Q1052545|lt=Osaka Line}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)|WD=Q17050860|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)}}
==References== {{reflist}}

align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|29|56|N|135|50|52|E}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Mesuriyama Kofun'''|メスリ山古墳|}} is an early [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofun]], located in the Takada neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Sakurai, Nara|en|Sakurai, Nara|WD=Q127026|lt=Sakurai, Nara|1=Sakurai, Nara}} in the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region|1=Kansai region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1980.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2036|title=メスリ山古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref>
==Overview== The Mesuriyama Kofun is located on the left bank of the Hatsuse River and belongs to the Torimiyama Kofun Cluster along with the [[Sakurai Chausuyama Kofun]], which has a separate National Historic Site designation. The Mesuriyama Kofun was excavated from 1959 to 1962. The tumulus is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It is orientated to the west, and has a total length of 224 meters. The posterior circular portion has a diameter of 128 meters and height of 19 meters. The anterior rectangular portion has a width of 80 meters at the front and a height of 8 meters. The slopes of the three-tiered mound is covered with head-sized ''[[fukiishi]]'' roofing stones, and rows of ''{{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}}'' in between each tier. In the center of the circular mound is a double square section, with huge ''haniwa'' clay figures in the shape of high stands and vessel platforms arranged perpendicular to the main axis of the mound, and a staggered arrangement of ''haniwa'' clay figures between the two rows, creating a distinctive layout. These pedestal-type ''haniwa'' are 2.4 meters high and 1.3 meters in diameter, and are the largest in Japan. Inside this row of ''haniwa'' clay figures is a square platform, beneath which is the main [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]], a pit-style stone chamber.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The burial chamber is approximately 8.06 meters long, 1.18 meters wide, and 1.76 meters high, and is covered by eight ceiling stones. A wooden coffin was placed in the burial chamber, but due to grave robbery, almost no remains remain. {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods|1=Grave goods}} unearthed in the main chamber included fragments of a {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|WD=Q1041984|lt=Bronze mirror|1=Bronze mirror}} (a triangular-rimmed divine-animal mirror), jadeite ''[[magatama]]'', jasper tubular beads, stone bracelets imitating shell rings, miniature stone chairs, combs, and bowls. The secondary burial chamber next to the main chamber is 6 meters long, 70 centimeters wide, and 60-70 centimeters high. There were no remains inside, only grave goods, and it is thought to have been a storage facility or a repository for the personal belongings of the deceased. It escaped grave robbery. many artifacts were found to be in perfect preservation.<ref name="Shiseki"/> These grave goods included 212 tipped iron spears, about half of which were shaped so that their tips faced each other at either end of the stone chamber. All of them are thought to have had long handles. They were weapons used in group battles. Iron spearheads with iron sword-shaped tips have also been excavated in the southern part of the {{ill|Korea|en|Korea|WD=Q18097|lt=Korea|1=Korea}} and in northern [[Kyushu]], and were the main weapon of the time. This weapon was very popular in the [[Japanese archipelago]], and there is no doubt that forging techniques were also used in Japan. The hoard also included 236 bronze arrowheads, 50 stone arrowheads, one iron bow (182 cm long, with an iron string), five iron arrows (80 cm long), and a lacquered shield. The iron bow and arrows were intended for intimidation purposes, not for practical use. Two iron swords, 14 axes, a 19 iron sickles, a chisel, and agricultural tools such as an awl, a knife, and a saw were also among the grave goods. These artifacts have been collectively designated as a {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/202763|title=奈良県メスリ山古墳出土品|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref>

Judging from the size of the mound, the size of the ''haniwa'', and the richness of the grave goods, it is believed to be the tomb of a chieftain with great power, but there us no mention of this ''kofun'' as being a royal tomb in historical documentation such as the ''[[Kojiki]]'' or ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tumulus is about 1.7 kilometers due south from {{ill|Sakurai Station (Nara)|en|Sakurai Station (Nara)|WD=Q5361714|lt=Sakurai Station|1=Sakurai Station (Nara)}} on the {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|en|Kintetsu Railway|WD=Q1531085|lt=Kintetsu Railway|1=Kintetsu Railway}} {{ill|Osaka Line|en|Osaka Line|WD=Q1052545|lt=Osaka Line|1=Osaka Line}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|{{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|29|56|N|135|50|52|E}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Mesuriyama Kofun'''|メスリ山古墳|}} is an early [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofun]], located in the Takada neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Sakurai, Nara|en|Sakurai, Nara|WD=Q127026|lt=Sakurai, Nara|1=Sakurai, Nara}} in the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region|1=Kansai region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1980.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2036|title=メスリ山古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref>
==Overview== The Mesuriyama Kofun is located on the left bank of the Hatsuse River and belongs to the Torimiyama Kofun Cluster along with the [[Sakurai Chausuyama Kofun]], which has a separate National Historic Site designation. The Mesuriyama Kofun was excavated from 1959 to 1962. The tumulus is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It is orientated to the west, and has a total length of 224 meters. The posterior circular portion has a diameter of 128 meters and height of 19 meters. The anterior rectangular portion has a width of 80 meters at the front and a height of 8 meters. The slopes of the three-tiered mound is covered with head-sized ''[[fukiishi]]'' roofing stones, and rows of ''{{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}}'' in between each tier. In the center of the circular mound is a double square section, with huge ''haniwa'' clay figures in the shape of high stands and vessel platforms arranged perpendicular to the main axis of the mound, and a staggered arrangement of ''haniwa'' clay figures between the two rows, creating a distinctive layout. These pedestal-type ''haniwa'' are 2.4 meters high and 1.3 meters in diameter, and are the largest in Japan. Inside this row of ''haniwa'' clay figures is a square platform, beneath which is the main [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]], a pit-style stone chamber.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The burial chamber is approximately 8.06 meters long, 1.18 meters wide, and 1.76 meters high, and is covered by eight ceiling stones. A wooden coffin was placed in the burial chamber, but due to grave robbery, almost no remains remain. {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods|1=Grave goods}} unearthed in the main chamber included fragments of a {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|WD=Q1041984|lt=Bronze mirror|1=Bronze mirror}} (a triangular-rimmed divine-animal mirror), jadeite ''[[magatama]]'', jasper tubular beads, stone bracelets imitating shell rings, miniature stone chairs, combs, and bowls. The secondary burial chamber next to the main chamber is 6 meters long, 70 centimeters wide, and 60-70 centimeters high. There were no remains inside, only grave goods, and it is thought to have been a storage facility or a repository for the personal belongings of the deceased. It escaped grave robbery. many artifacts were found to be in perfect preservation.<ref name="Shiseki"/> These grave goods included 212 tipped iron spears, about half of which were shaped so that their tips faced each other at either end of the stone chamber. All of them are thought to have had long handles. They were weapons used in group battles. Iron spearheads with iron sword-shaped tips have also been excavated in the southern part of the {{ill|Korea|en|Korea|WD=Q18097|lt=Korea|1=Korea}} and in northern [[Kyushu]], and were the main weapon of the time. This weapon was very popular in the [[Japanese archipelago]], and there is no doubt that forging techniques were also used in Japan. The hoard also included 236 bronze arrowheads, 50 stone arrowheads, one iron bow (182 cm long, with an iron string), five iron arrows (80 cm long), and a lacquered shield. The iron bow and arrows were intended for intimidation purposes, not for practical use. Two iron swords, 14 axes, a 19 iron sickles, a chisel, and agricultural tools such as an awl, a knife, and a saw were also among the grave goods. These artifacts have been collectively designated as a {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/202763|title=奈良県メスリ山古墳出土品|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref>
<gallery> Mesuriyama kohun aerial.jpg|Aerial view File:Mesuriyama Kofun, funcho.jpg|The top of the circular mound File:Mesuriyama Kofun, kouenbu.jpg|View of the circular mound from the front File:Mesuriyama Kofun, zenpoubu.jpg|View of the front part from the circular mound メスリ山古墳出土 大型円筒埴輪-2.JPG|Large cylindrical ''haniwa'' at the Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute </gallery>
Judging from the size of the mound, the size of the ''haniwa'', and the richness of the grave goods, it is believed to be the tomb of a chieftain with great power, but there us no mention of this ''kofun'' as being a royal tomb in historical documentation such as the ''[[Kojiki]]'' or ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tumulus is about 1.7 kilometers due south from {{ill|Sakurai Station (Nara)|en|Sakurai Station (Nara)|WD=Q5361714|lt=Sakurai Station|1=Sakurai Station (Nara)}} on the {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|en|Kintetsu Railway|WD=Q1531085|lt=Kintetsu Railway|1=Kintetsu Railway}} {{ill|Osaka Line|en|Osaka Line|WD=Q1052545|lt=Osaka Line|1=Osaka Line}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)|WD=Q17050860|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)|1=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)}}
==References== {{reflist}}

Judging from the size of the mound, the size of the ''haniwa'', and the richness of the grave goods, it is believed to be the tomb of a chieftain with great power, but there us no mention of this ''kofun'' as being a royal tomb in historical documentation such as the ''[[Kojiki]]'' or ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tumulus is about 1.7 kilometers due south from {{ill|Sakurai Station (Nara)|en|Sakurai Station (Nara)|WD=Q5361714|lt=Sakurai Station|1=Sakurai Station (Nara)}} on the {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|en|Kintetsu Railway|WD=Q1531085|lt=Kintetsu Railway|1=Kintetsu Railway}} {{ill|Osaka Line|en|Osaka Line|WD=Q1052545|lt=Osaka Line|1=Osaka Line}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)|WD=Q17050860|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)|1=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)}}
==References== {{reflist}}

Kofun]][[ja:メスリ山古墳]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Mesuriyama Kofun]][[ja:メスリ山古墳]] {{Short description|Kofun period keyhole-shaped Kofun in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] | name =Mesuriyama Kofun | native_name =メスリ山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Mesuriyama Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Mesuriyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Nara Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Mesuriyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34|29|56|N|135|50|52|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = {{ill|Sakurai, Nara|en|Sakurai, Nara|WD=Q127026|lt=Sakurai, Nara|1=Sakurai, Nara}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} | region = {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region|1=Kansai region}} | type = Kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.4th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|{{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|29|56|N|135|50|52|E}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Mesuriyama Kofun'''|メスリ山古墳|}} is an early [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofun]], located in the Takada neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Sakurai, Nara|en|Sakurai, Nara|WD=Q127026|lt=Sakurai, Nara|1=Sakurai, Nara}} in the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region|1=Kansai region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1980.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2036|title=メスリ山古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref>
==Overview== The Mesuriyama Kofun is located on the left bank of the Hatsuse River and belongs to the Torimiyama Kofun Cluster along with the [[Sakurai Chausuyama Kofun]], which has a separate National Historic Site designation. The Mesuriyama Kofun was excavated from 1959 to 1962. The tumulus is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It is orientated to the west, and has a total length of 224 meters. The posterior circular portion has a diameter of 128 meters and height of 19 meters. The anterior rectangular portion has a width of 80 meters at the front and a height of 8 meters. The slopes of the three-tiered mound is covered with head-sized ''[[fukiishi]]'' roofing stones, and rows of ''{{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}}'' in between each tier. In the center of the circular mound is a double square section, with huge ''haniwa'' clay figures in the shape of high stands and vessel platforms arranged perpendicular to the main axis of the mound, and a staggered arrangement of ''haniwa'' clay figures between the two rows, creating a distinctive layout. These pedestal-type ''haniwa'' are 2.4 meters high and 1.3 meters in diameter, and are the largest in Japan. Inside this row of ''haniwa'' clay figures is a square platform, beneath which is the main [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]], a pit-style stone chamber.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The burial chamber is approximately 8.06 meters long, 1.18 meters wide, and 1.76 meters high, and is covered by eight ceiling stones. A wooden coffin was placed in the burial chamber, but due to grave robbery, almost no remains remain. {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods|1=Grave goods}} unearthed in the main chamber included fragments of a {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|WD=Q1041984|lt=Bronze mirror|1=Bronze mirror}} (a triangular-rimmed divine-animal mirror), jadeite ''[[magatama]]'', jasper tubular beads, stone bracelets imitating shell rings, miniature stone chairs, combs, and bowls. The secondary burial chamber next to the main chamber is 6 meters long, 70 centimeters wide, and 60-70 centimeters high. There were no remains inside, only grave goods, and it is thought to have been a storage facility or a repository for the personal belongings of the deceased. It escaped grave robbery. many artifacts were found to be in perfect preservation.<ref name="Shiseki"/> These grave goods included 212 tipped iron spears, about half of which were shaped so that their tips faced each other at either end of the stone chamber. All of them are thought to have had long handles. They were weapons used in group battles. Iron spearheads with iron sword-shaped tips have also been excavated in the southern part of the {{ill|Korea|en|Korea|WD=Q18097|lt=Korea|1=Korea}} and in northern [[Kyushu]], and were the main weapon of the time. This weapon was very popular in the [[Japanese archipelago]], and there is no doubt that forging techniques were also used in Japan. The hoard also included 236 bronze arrowheads, 50 stone arrowheads, one iron bow (182 cm long, with an iron string), five iron arrows (80 cm long), and a lacquered shield. The iron bow and arrows were intended for intimidation purposes, not for practical use. Two iron swords, 14 axes, a 19 iron sickles, a chisel, and agricultural tools such as an awl, a knife, and a saw were also among the grave goods. These artifacts have been collectively designated as a {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/202763|title=奈良県メスリ山古墳出土品|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref>
<gallery> Mesuriyama kohun aerial.jpg|Aerial view File:Mesuriyama Kofun, funcho.jpg|The top of the circular mound File:Mesuriyama Kofun, kouenbu.jpg|View of the circular mound from the front File:Mesuriyama Kofun, zenpoubu.jpg|View of the front part from the circular mound メスリ山古墳出土 大型円筒埴輪-2.JPG|Large cylindrical ''haniwa'' at the Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute </gallery>
Judging from the size of the mound, the size of the ''haniwa'', and the richness of the grave goods, it is believed to be the tomb of a chieftain with great power, but there us no mention of this ''kofun'' as being a royal tomb in historical documentation such as the ''[[Kojiki]]'' or ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tumulus is about 1.7 kilometers due south from {{ill|Sakurai Station (Nara)|en|Sakurai Station (Nara)|WD=Q5361714|lt=Sakurai Station|1=Sakurai Station (Nara)}} on the {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|en|Kintetsu Railway|WD=Q1531085|lt=Kintetsu Railway|1=Kintetsu Railway}} {{ill|Osaka Line|en|Osaka Line|WD=Q1052545|lt=Osaka Line|1=Osaka Line}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)|WD=Q17050860|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)|1=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)}}

The anterior rectangular portion has a width of 80 meters at the front and a height of 8 meters. The slopes of the three-tiered mound is covered with head-sized ''[[fukiishi]]'' roofing stones, and rows of ''{{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|qid=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}}'' in between each tier. In the center of the circular mound is a double square section, with huge ''haniwa'' clay figures in the shape of high stands and vessel platforms arranged perpendicular to the main axis of the mound, and a staggered arrangement of ''haniwa'' clay figures between the two rows, creating a distinctive layout. These pedestal-type ''haniwa'' are 2.4 meters high and 1.3 meters in diameter, and are the largest in Japan. Inside this row of ''haniwa'' clay figures is a square platform, beneath which is the main [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]], a pit-style stone chamber.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The burial chamber is approximately 8.06 meters long, 1.18 meters wide, and 1.76 meters high, and is covered by eight ceiling stones. A wooden coffin was placed in the burial chamber, but due to grave robbery, almost no remains remain. {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|qid=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods|1=Grave goods}} unearthed in the main chamber included fragments of a {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|qid=Q1041984|lt=Bronze mirror|1=Bronze mirror}} (a triangular-rimmed divine-animal mirror), jadeite ''[[magatama]]'', jasper tubular beads, stone bracelets imitating shell rings, miniature stone chairs, combs, and bowls. The secondary burial chamber next to the main chamber is 6 meters long, 70 centimeters wide, and 60-70 centimeters high. There were no remains inside, only grave goods, and it is thought to have been a storage facility or a repository for the personal belongings of the deceased. It escaped grave robbery. many artifacts were found to be in perfect preservation.<ref name="Shiseki"/> These grave goods included 212 tipped iron spears, about half of which were shaped so that their tips faced each other at either end of the stone chamber. All of them are thought to have had long handles. They were weapons used in group battles. Iron spearheads with iron sword-shaped tips have also been excavated in the southern part of the {{ill|Korea|en|Korea|qid=Q18097|lt=Korea|1=Korea}} and in northern [[Kyushu]], and were the main weapon of the time. This weapon was very popular in the [[Japanese archipelago]], and there is no doubt that forging techniques were also used in Japan. The hoard also included 236 bronze arrowheads, 50 stone arrowheads, one iron bow (182 cm long, with an iron string), five iron arrows (80 cm long), and a lacquered shield. The iron bow and arrows were intended for intimidation purposes, not for practical use. Two iron swords, 14 axes, a 19 iron sickles, a chisel, and agricultural tools such as an awl, a knife, and a saw were also among the grave goods. These artifacts have been collectively designated as a {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|qid=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/202763|title=奈良県メスリ山古墳出土品|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref>
<gallery> Mesuriyama kohun aerial.jpg|Aerial view File:Mesuriyama Kofun, funcho.jpg|The top of the circular mound File:Mesuriyama Kofun, kouenbu.jpg|View of the circular mound from the front File:Mesuriyama Kofun, zenpoubu.jpg|View of the front part from the circular mound メスリ山古墳出土 大型円筒埴輪-2.JPG|Large cylindrical ''haniwa'' at the Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute </gallery>
Judging from the size of the mound, the size of the ''haniwa'', and the richness of the grave goods, it is believed to be the tomb of a chieftain with great power, but there us no mention of this ''kofun'' as being a royal tomb in historical documentation such as the ''[[Kojiki]]'' or ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tumulus is about 1.7 kilometers due south from {{ill|Sakurai Station (Nara)|en|Sakurai Station (Nara)|qid=Q5361714|lt=Sakurai Station|1=Sakurai Station (Nara)}} on the {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|en|Kintetsu Railway|qid=Q1531085|lt=Kintetsu Railway|1=Kintetsu Railway}} {{ill|Osaka Line|en|Osaka Line|qid=Q1052545|lt=Osaka Line|1=Osaka Line}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)|qid=Q17050860|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)|1=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)}}

align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|29|56|N|135|50|52|E}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Mesuriyama Kofun'''|メスリ山古墳|}} is an early [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofun]], located in the Takada neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Sakurai, Nara|en|Sakurai, Nara|qid=Q127026|lt=Sakurai, Nara|1=Sakurai, Nara}} in the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|qid=Q164256|lt=Kansai region|1=Kansai region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1980.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2036|title=メスリ山古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref>
==Overview== The Mesuriyama Kofun is located on the left bank of the Hatsuse River and belongs to the Torimiyama Kofun Cluster along with the [[Sakurai Chausuyama Kofun]], which has a separate National Historic Site designation. The Mesuriyama Kofun was excavated from 1959 to 1962. The tumulus is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpō-kōen-fun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpō-kōen-fun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpō-kōen-fun|1=Zenpō-kōen-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpō-kōen-fun|1=[[Zenpō-kōen-fun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It is orientated to the west, and has a total length of 224 meters. The posterior circular portion has a diameter of 128 meters and height of 19 meters. The anterior rectangular portion has a width of 80 meters at the front and a height of 8 meters. The slopes of the three-tiered mound is covered with head-sized ''[[fukiishi]]'' roofing stones, and rows of ''{{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|qid=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}}'' in between each tier. In the center of the circular mound is a double square section, with huge ''haniwa'' clay figures in the shape of high stands and vessel platforms arranged perpendicular to the main axis of the mound, and a staggered arrangement of ''haniwa'' clay figures between the two rows, creating a distinctive layout. These pedestal-type ''haniwa'' are 2.4 meters high and 1.3 meters in diameter, and are the largest in Japan. Inside this row of ''haniwa'' clay figures is a square platform, beneath which is the main [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]], a pit-style stone chamber.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The burial chamber is approximately 8.06 meters long, 1.18 meters wide, and 1.76 meters high, and is covered by eight ceiling stones. A wooden coffin was placed in the burial chamber, but due to grave robbery, almost no remains remain. {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|qid=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods|1=Grave goods}} unearthed in the main chamber included fragments of a {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|qid=Q1041984|lt=Bronze mirror|1=Bronze mirror}} (a triangular-rimmed divine-animal mirror), jadeite ''[[magatama]]'', jasper tubular beads, stone bracelets imitating shell rings, miniature stone chairs, combs, and bowls. The secondary burial chamber next to the main chamber is 6 meters long, 70 centimeters wide, and 60-70 centimeters high. There were no remains inside, only grave goods, and it is thought to have been a storage facility or a repository for the personal belongings of the deceased. It escaped grave robbery. many artifacts were found to be in perfect preservation.<ref name="Shiseki"/> These grave goods included 212 tipped iron spears, about half of which were shaped so that their tips faced each other at either end of the stone chamber. All of them are thought to have had long handles. They were weapons used in group battles. Iron spearheads with iron sword-shaped tips have also been excavated in the southern part of the {{ill|Korea|en|Korea|qid=Q18097|lt=Korea|1=Korea}} and in northern [[Kyushu]], and were the main weapon of the time. This weapon was very popular in the [[Japanese archipelago]], and there is no doubt that forging techniques were also used in Japan. The hoard also included 236 bronze arrowheads, 50 stone arrowheads, one iron bow (182 cm long, with an iron string), five iron arrows (80 cm long), and a lacquered shield. The iron bow and arrows were intended for intimidation purposes, not for practical use. Two iron swords, 14 axes, a 19 iron sickles, a chisel, and agricultural tools such as an awl, a knife, and a saw were also among the grave goods. These artifacts have been collectively designated as a {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|qid=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/202763|title=奈良県メスリ山古墳出土品|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref>

Judging from the size of the mound, the size of the ''haniwa'', and the richness of the grave goods, it is believed to be the tomb of a chieftain with great power, but there us no mention of this ''kofun'' as being a royal tomb in historical documentation such as the ''[[Kojiki]]'' or ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tumulus is about 1.7 kilometers due south from {{ill|Sakurai Station (Nara)|en|Sakurai Station (Nara)|qid=Q5361714|lt=Sakurai Station|1=Sakurai Station (Nara)}} on the {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|en|Kintetsu Railway|qid=Q1531085|lt=Kintetsu Railway|1=Kintetsu Railway}} {{ill|Osaka Line|en|Osaka Line|qid=Q1052545|lt=Osaka Line|1=Osaka Line}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
